# Transcrição: 06 MU2-Module 6_343_Stegner ReunionFinal

## [00:00 - 03:01]

When adversity hits, when things go wrong, what's the difference between those who find their inner strength and succeed against all odds and those who become weak, give up and lose everything? Some may say it is intelligence, some say it is hard work, and these are very important qualities, but even more important is love, the ability to give love and to feel loved by others. In this film you will meet Ron, whose life had just hit rock bottom after he lost his job, lost his life savings in a start-up business, and was quickly running out of money for the mortgage. He was also quickly losing the love and respect of his wife. When Ron first met Tony, he was distraught, empty and nearly broken. Tony saw that beneath Ron's career and financial challenges lay a more serious and fundamental problem. Ron's relationships had put him in so much pain and personal distress that he was robbed of his true vitality, creativity and initiative. In other words, he had lost his sources of love. Since meeting them a few weeks earlier, Tony took this couple through a dramatic process that healed their marriage. Ron recovered his wife's love and regained the inner strength he would need to change his finances. But what Tony also learned along the way was that Ron had another deep relationship pain, this one with his parents whom he loved very much. Four years earlier, after the birth of his first child, an argument between Ron's mother and his wife had led him to cut off communications with his parents. And this caused him pain on a daily basis. Ron and Marie had never mentioned any of this to Tony, but Tony knew this pain needed to be healed. Tony is about to surprise Ron by reuniting him with his estranged parents. Tony will demonstrate how this family can overcome hurt feelings and restore the love that had been lost. You'll see how Ron, with both of his main sources of love restored, can reclaim the inner strength he needs to reshape his future. Let's join Tony as he surprises Ron and Marie and leads them to reunite with Ron's parents. Well, hello! Everywhere! I'm haunting you! How are you? This is good, I'm bored, but look! That's always good in the end, right? How are you doing, buddy? Good, how are you doing? Tell me about your family. Now, my parents, I've always been very close to my parents. Unfortunately, over the last couple of years, we had some differences and we have not been communicating at all.

Here's an example of a typical difference in family culture. Marie's family believes in explicit communication. If something bothers you, you bring it out in the open and settle your differences right away. Ron's family believes in implicit communication, meaning that if there are tensions, you ignore them and focus on the joy of the children and your togetherness. Neither approach is right or wrong, but you can see how easily a misunderstanding could take place. Ron's parents probably felt that Marie was constantly bringing up unpleasant questions that are best left alone. Marie probably felt that Ron's parents were avoiding communicating their criticisms of her. And Ron felt unequipped to reconcile these different family cultures. He tried to please his parents' culture by focusing on joy instead of communicating his wife's feelings and needs, and therefore his parents never learned what was going on. And unable to please his wife with this approach, he decided the best solution was to sever communications.

Anything? Anything? Definitely. So, hand in the middle of never again this round. In the middle. Straight on. Yeah, reach it. Spare my heel. Okay. That's what love has brought together with grace. When you have seriously harmed a relationship in your family, even if the pain is unintentional, as in Ron's case, the most healing thing you can do for your family is to apologize. When you take responsibility for the choices that you have made that have harmed others in your family, it restores the family structure, lets people understand that you care and that they are honored, respects your own good intentions and the good intentions of others, and it opens up new ways to communicate. When apologizing, it is important that you speak from the heart and that you don't make demands from the person to whom you're apologizing. Don't ask forgiveness. Simply apologize. As you can see, Ron's heartfelt apology also created an empowering meaning that enabled this family to rebuild their relationship in a whole new way. Thank you.
